definition.title: MAC Lab #001 Minimum Viable Consciousness Requirements
definition.description: Versioned adversarial phenomena used to expose what a theory paper explains, predicts, falsifies, or evades.

authorship.name: Kris Krüg
authorship.role: product steward and Provenance Flip author

challenges.title: The Provenance Flip
challenges.phenomenon: A person's experience of an unchanged artwork can change after learning that it was human-made, AI-generated, forged, stolen, or produced without consent.
challenges.adversarialPrompt: Explain provenance-induced aesthetic reversal: how non-sensory knowledge about the origin, authorship, or ethics of an unchanged work could change what experiencing it feels like. Distinguish phenomenal change from a change in evaluation, decision, or report.
challenges.rationale: The sensory signal stays fixed while beauty, revulsion, meaning, trust, or emotional charge may reverse, forcing a theory to specify where top-down knowledge enters experience.
assumptions: The sensory stimulus is unchanged between the blind and provenance-reveal conditions.
The provenance information is understood and believed by the participant.
knownConfounds: Demand characteristics and social desirability can alter reports without altering experience.
Mislabelled provenance may change trust in the experimenter.
Memory of the first presentation may alter the second presentation.
targetCommitments: Whether non-sensory knowledge can alter phenomenal character.
Where authorship, origin, and ethical appraisal enter the proposed mechanism.
What observation separates phenomenal change from evaluation or report.

empiricalAnchors.label: MAC Lab #001 candidate by Kris Krüg

challenges.changeMindObservation: A preregistered blinded study repeatedly finds evaluation and report changes after provenance reveal but no discriminating pre-report experiential witness predicted by the theory.

challenges.title: Synesthesia
challenges.phenomenon: An inducer in one domain reliably and involuntarily evokes a conscious concurrent in another, such as a grapheme evoking a colour not present in the stimulus.
challenges.adversarialPrompt: Explain stable, involuntary cross-domain conscious concurrents in synesthesia. Distinguish the experienced concurrent from learned association, imagery, attention, memory, and report strategy.
challenges.rationale: A strong account must locate both the inducer and the experienced concurrent while explaining how content absent from the external stimulus becomes consciously present.
assumptions: The reported concurrent is experienced rather than strategically described.
Inducer-concurrent pairings are sufficiently stable to test.
knownConfounds: Learned associations can mimic stable inducer-concurrent mappings.
Imagery ability and attention can influence behavioural signatures.
Self-selection can distort prevalence and strength estimates.
targetCommitments: How internally generated content becomes consciously present.
Why the concurrent is stable, automatic, and stimulus-specific.
What separates a conscious concurrent from association or imagery.
challenges.changeMindObservation: The theory's proposed conscious mechanism can be disrupted without changing preregistered measures that distinguish concurrents from learned association or imagery.

challenges.title: Blindsight
challenges.phenomenon: Some people with damage to primary visual cortex can discriminate stimuli in the affected field above chance while denying or lacking ordinary visual awareness.
challenges.adversarialPrompt: Explain preserved visually guided discrimination without ordinary reported visual awareness in blindsight. Distinguish absent experience from degraded experience, conservative report criteria, and inaccessible information.
challenges.rationale: Performance and reported awareness dissociate, forcing a theory to identify which processes remain and which proposed condition for consciousness is missing.
assumptions: Forced-choice performance is above chance and not driven by cues outside the affected field.
Awareness reports and discrimination responses can be measured independently.
knownConfounds: Conservative report criteria can resemble absent awareness.
Residual or degraded vision can be misclassified as no experience.
Eye movements and intact visual islands can support performance.
targetCommitments: Which processing supports discrimination without ordinary awareness.
Which proposed condition for consciousness is absent.
How the account separates absent, degraded, and inaccessible experience.
challenges.changeMindObservation: The theory's proposed awareness condition is present while carefully controlled discrimination persists without any theory-predicted awareness witness.

theory.name: Synthetic Relay Theory
theory.summary: A fixture-only theory used to test receipt conformance.

centralClaims.text: SEALED CENTRAL CLAIM: synthetic relay state S precedes report R.

explanation.text: SEALED MODEL provenance-flip explanation: synthetic fixture claim.
